text:
Our visitor gave a rather shamefaced laugh. 'Shillings have not been so plentiful with me as they once were,' he remarked. 'I had no doubt that the gang of roughs who assaulted me had carried off both my hat and the bird. I did not care to spend more money in a hopeless attempt at recovering them.'  
'Very naturally. By the way, about the bird, we were compelled to eat it.'  
'To eat it!' Our visitor half rose from his chair in his excitement.  
'Yes, it would have been of no use to anyone had we not done so. But I presume that this other goose upon the sideboard, which is about the same weight and perfectly fresh, will answer your purpose equally well?'  
'Oh, certainly, certainly,' answered Mr. Baker with a sigh of relief.  
'Of course, we still have the feathers, legs, crop, and so on of your own bird, so if you wish-'  
The man burst into a hearty laugh. 'They might be useful to me as relics of my adventure,' said he, 'but beyond that I can hardly see what use the disjecta membra of my late acquaintance are going to be to me. No, sir, I think that, with your permission, I will confine my attentions to the excellent bird which I perceive upon the sideboard.'  
Sherlock Holmes glanced sharply across at me with a slight shrug of his shoulders.  
'There is your hat, then, and there your bird,' said he. 'By the way, would it bore you to tell me where you got the other one from? I am somewhat of a fowl fancier, and I have seldom seen a better grown goose.'  
'Certainly, sir,' said Baker, who had risen and tucked his newly gained property under his arm. 'There are a few of us who frequent the Alpha Inn, near the Museum-we are to be found in the Museum itself during the day, you understand. This year our good host, Windigate by name, instituted a goose club, by which, on consideration of some few pence every week, we were each to receive a bird at Christmas. My pence were duly paid, and the rest is familiar to you. I am much indebted to you, sir, for a Scotch bonnet is fitted neither to my years nor my gravity.' With a comical pomposity of manner he bowed solemnly to both of us and strode off upon his way.  
'So much for Mr. Henry Baker,' said Holmes when he had closed the door behind him. 'It is quite certain that he knows nothing whatever about the matter. Are you hungry, Watson?'  
'Not particularly.'  
'Then I suggest that we turn our dinner into a supper and follow up this clue while it is still hot.'  
'By all means.'  
It was a bitter night, so we drew on our ulsters and wrapped cravats about our throats. Outside, the stars were shining coldly in a cloudless sky, and the breath of the passers-by blew out into smoke like so many pistol shots. Our footfalls rang out crisply and loudly as we swung through the doctors' quarter, Wimpole Street, Harley Street, and so through Wigmore Street into Oxford Street. In a quarter of an hour we were in Bloomsbury at the Alpha Inn, which is a small public-house at the corner of one of the streets which runs down into Holborn. Holmes pushed open the door of the private bar and ordered two glasses of beer from the ruddy-faced, white-aproned landlord.  
'Your beer should be excellent if it is as good as your geese,' said he.  
'My geese!' The man seemed surprised.  
'Yes. I was speaking only half an hour ago to Mr. Henry Baker, who was a member of your goose club.'  
'Ah! yes, I see. But you see, sir, them's not our geese.'  
'Indeed! Whose, then?'  
'Well, I got the two dozen from a salesman in Covent Garden.'  
'Indeed? I know some of them. Which was it?'  
'Breckinridge is his name.'  
'Ah! I don't know him. Well, here's your good health landlord, and prosperity to your house. Goodnight.'  
'Now for Mr. Breckinridge,' he continued, buttoning up his coat as we came out into the frosty air. 'Remember, Watson that though we have so homely a thing as a goose at one end of this chain, we have at the other a man who will certainly get seven years' penal servitude unless we can establish his innocence. It is possible that our inquiry may but confirm his guilt; but, in any case, we have a line of investigation which has been missed by the police, and which a singular chance has placed in our hands. Let us follow it out to the bitter end. Faces to the south, then, and quick march!'  
We passed across Holborn, down Endell Street, and so through a zigzag of slums to Covent Garden Market. One of the largest stalls bore the name of Breckinridge upon it, and the proprietor a horseylooking man, with a sharp face and trim side-whiskers was helping a boy to put up the shutters.  
'Good-evening. It's a cold night,' said Holmes.  
The salesman nodded and shot a questioning glance at my companion.  
'Sold out of geese, I see,' continued Holmes, pointing at the bare slabs of marble.  
'Let you have five hundred to-morrow morning.' 'That's no good.'  
'Well, there are some on the stall with the gasflare.'  
'Ah, but I was recommended to you.'  
'Who by?'  
'The landlord of the Alpha.'  
'Oh, yes; I sent him a couple of dozen.'  
'Fine birds they were, too. Now where did you get them from?'  
To my surprise the question provoked a burst of anger from the salesman.  
'Now, then, mister,' said he, with his head cocked and his arms akimbo, 'what are you driving at? Let's have it straight, now.'  
'It is straight enough. I should like to know who sold you the geese which you supplied to the Alpha.'  
'Well then, I shan't tell you. So now!'  
'Oh, it is a matter of no importance; but I don't know why you should be so warm over such a trifle.'  
'Warm! You'd be as warm, maybe, if you were as pestered as I am. When I pay good money for a good article there should be an end of the business; but it's 'Where are the geese?' and 'Who did you sell the geese to?' and 'What will you take for the geese?' One would think they were the only geese in the world, to hear the fuss that is made over them.'  
'Well, I have no connection with any other people who have been making inquiries,' said Holmes carelessly. 'If you won't tell us the bet is off, that is all. But I'm always ready to back my opinion on a matter of fowls, and I have a fiver on it that the bird I ate is country bred.'  
'Well, then, you've lost your fiver, for it's town bred,' snapped the salesman.  
'It's nothing of the kind.'  
'I say it is.'  
'I don't believe it.'  
'D'you think you know more about fowls than I, who have handled them ever since I was a nipper? I tell you, all those birds that went to the Alpha were town bred.'  
'You'll never persuade me to believe that.'  
'Will you bet, then?'  
'It's merely taking your money, for I know that I am right. But I'll have a sovereign on with you, just to teach you not to be obstinate.'  
The salesman chuckled grimly. 'Bring me the books, Bill,' said he.  
The small boy brought round a small thin volume and a great greasy-backed one, laying them out together beneath the hanging lamp.  
'Now then, Mr. Cocksure,' said the salesman, 'I thought that I was out of geese, but before I finish you'll find that there is still one left in my shop. You see this little book?'  
'Well?'  
'That's the list of the folk from whom I buy. D'you see? Well, then, here on this page are the country folk, and the numbers after their names are where their accounts are in the big ledger. Now, then! You see this other page in red ink? Well, that is a list of my town suppliers. Now, look at that third name. Just read it out to me.'
name: Chunk 4/7

entities.name: The Wager
entities.description: A heated bet between Holmes and Breckinridge regarding whether the geese were country or town bred. This wager forces the salesman to reveal his supply ledger, advancing the investigation.
context: 'I'm always ready to back my opinion on a matter of fowls, and I have a fiver on it that the bird I ate is country bred.'
'Well, then, you've lost your fiver, for it's town bred,' snapped the salesman.
'I'll have a sovereign on with you, just to teach you not to be obstinate.'
'Bring me the books, Bill,' said he.

entities.name: The Investigation
entities.description: Holmes and Watson's methodical pursuit of clues from the Alpha Inn to Covent Garden Market, driven by the desire to clear a man's name and solve a theft that evaded the police.
context: 'Then I suggest that we turn our dinner into a supper and follow up this clue while it is still hot.'
'It is possible that our inquiry may but confirm his guilt; but, in any case, we have a line of investigation which has been missed by the police...'
'Let us follow it out to the bitter end. Faces to the south, then, and quick march!'
'Now for Mr. Breckinridge,' he continued, buttoning up his coat as we came out into the frosty air.

entities.name: The Goose Club
entities.description: A social group formed by patrons of the Alpha Inn who pool small weekly payments to receive a goose at Christmas. It serves as the social framework connecting Baker, Windigate, and the stolen birds.
context: 'This year our good host, Windigate by name, instituted a goose club, by which, on consideration of some few pence every week, we were each to receive a bird at Christmas.'
'Yes. I was speaking only half an hour ago to Mr. Henry Baker, who was a member of your goose club.'
'Ah! yes, I see. But you see, sir, them's not our geese.'

entities.name: The Police
entities.description: The official law enforcement body responsible for the initial investigation, whose methods Holmes considers incomplete and in need of correction through his own deductive approach.
context: 'we have a line of investigation which has been missed by the police, and which a singular chance has placed in our hands.'
'So much for Mr. Henry Baker,' said Holmes when he had closed the door behind him. 'It is quite certain that he knows nothing whatever about the matter.'

entities.name: Penal Servitude
entities.description: The severe legal punishment facing the thief, highlighting the high stakes of the investigation and the moral imperative for Holmes to establish the culprit's innocence or guilt.
context: 'Remember, Watson that though we have so homely a thing as a goose at one end of this chain, we have at the other a man who will certainly get seven years' penal servitude unless we can establish his innocence.'
'It is possible that our inquiry may but confirm his guilt; but, in any case, we have a line of investigation which has been missed by the police...'

entities.name: Fowl Origin Classification
entities.description: The debate over whether the stolen geese originated from the countryside or the city, serving as a crucial deductive clue that forces the revelation of the supply ledger.
context: 'I'm always ready to back my opinion on a matter of fowls, and I have a fiver on it that the bird I ate is country bred.'
'Well, then, you've lost your fiver, for it's town bred,' snapped the salesman.
'I tell you, all those birds that went to the Alpha were town bred.'
'You'll never persuade me to believe that.'

entities.name: Legal Morality
entities.description: The central moral and legal dilemma driving the investigation, as Holmes seeks to determine whether the thief deserves severe punishment or can be proven innocent.
context: 'unless we can establish his innocence.'
'It is possible that our inquiry may but confirm his guilt; but, in any case, we have a line of investigation which has been missed by the police...'
'So much for Mr. Henry Baker,' said Holmes when he had closed the door behind him. 'It is quite certain that he knows nothing whatever about the matter.'

properties.cause: Stolen property and mystery of the goose's origin

properties.purpose/goal: Investigate the theft and pursue the guilty party

relationships.description: Baker acknowledges Holmes's assistance and expresses gratitude for the return of his property.
relationships.context: I am much indebted to you, sir, for a Scotch bonnet is fitted neither to my years nor my gravity.

relationships.description: Baker was the victim of an assault by a gang of roughs who stole his hat and goose.
relationships.context: I had no doubt that the gang of roughs who assaulted me had carried off both my hat and the bird.

relationships.description: Baker frequents the Alpha Inn as a regular patron and member of the local goose club.
relationships.context: There are a few of us who frequent the Alpha Inn, near the Museum-we are to be found in the Museum itself during the day, you understand.

relationships.description: Baker is a paying member of the Goose Club, which provides him with a bird at Christmas.
relationships.context: This year our good host, Windigate by name, instituted a goose club, by which, on consideration of some few pence every week, we were each to receive a bird at Christmas.

relationships.description: Baker owns the Scotch bonnet hat that was stolen during the assault.
relationships.context: I am much indebted to you, sir, for a Scotch bonnet is fitted neither to my years nor my gravity.

relationships.description: Holmes takes on the case to investigate the assault and theft involving Baker's property.
relationships.context: Then I suggest that we turn our dinner into a supper and follow up this clue while it is still hot.

relationships.description: Holmes leads the broader investigation into the origin of the geese and the underlying crime.
relationships.context: but we have a line of investigation which has been missed by the police, and which a singular chance has placed in our hands.

relationships.description: Holmes collaborates closely with Watson, treating him as a companion and assistant in the case.
relationships.context: So much for Mr. Henry Baker,' said Holmes when he had closed the door behind him. 'It is quite certain that he knows nothing whatever about the matter. Are you hungry, Watson?

relationships.description: Holmes visits the Alpha Inn to question the landlord about the goose supply chain.
relationships.context: In a quarter of an hour we were in Bloomsbury at the Alpha Inn, which is a small public-house at the corner of one of the streets which runs down into Holborn.

relationships.description: Holmes travels to Covent Garden Market to track down the salesman Breckinridge.
relationships.context: We passed across Holborn, down Endell Street, and so through a zigzag of slums to Covent Garden Market.

relationships.description: Holmes engages in a bet with Breckinridge regarding the origin of the geese.

properties.contribution: Staked a fiver on country-bred origin

relationships.context: If you won't tell us the bet is off, that is all. But I'm always ready to back my opinion on a matter of fowls, and I have a fiver on it that the bird I ate is country bred.

relationships.description: Watson accompanies Holmes to the Alpha Inn during their investigation.
relationships.context: In a quarter of an hour we were in Bloomsbury at the Alpha Inn, which is a small public-house at the corner of one of the streets which runs down into Holborn.

relationships.description: Watson follows Holmes to Covent Garden Market to observe the interrogation of Breckinridge.
relationships.context: We passed across Holborn, down Endell Street, and so through a zigzag of slums to Covent Garden Market.

relationships.description: Watson assists Holmes in the broader investigation by accompanying him and providing support.
relationships.context: Then I suggest that we turn our dinner into a supper and follow up this clue while it is still hot.

relationships.description: Windigate founded and operates the Goose Club, which supplies members with Christmas geese.
relationships.context: This year our good host, Windigate by name, instituted a goose club, by which, on consideration of some few pence every week, we were each to receive a bird at Christmas.

relationships.description: Windigate is the landlord and proprietor of the Alpha Inn.
relationships.context: Holmes pushed open the door of the private bar and ordered two glasses of beer from the ruddy-faced, white-aproned landlord.

relationships.description: Breckinridge operates a stall at Covent Garden Market as a salesman.
relationships.context: One of the largest stalls bore the name of Breckinridge upon it, and the proprietor a horseylooking man, with a sharp face and trim side-whiskers was helping a boy to put up the shutters.

relationships.description: Breckinridge accepts Holmes's bet regarding the geographical origin of the geese.

properties.contribution: Staked a sovereign on town-bred origin

relationships.context: 'Will you bet, then?' 'It is merely taking your money, for I know that I am right. But I'll have a sovereign on with you, just to teach you not to be obstinate.'

relationships.description: Bill assists Breckinridge as a young boy at his market stall.
relationships.context: The salesman nodded and shot a questioning glance at my companion. ... 'Bring me the books, Bill,' said he. The small boy brought round a small thin volume and a great greasy-backed one...

relationships.description: Baker is a key witness in the investigation, though he initially knows nothing of the underlying matter.

properties.contribution: Provides initial clue via stolen property

relationships.context: 'It is quite certain that he knows nothing whatever about the matter.

relationships.description: Breckinridge maintains detailed ledgers tracking his suppliers and their accounts.
relationships.context: You see this little book?' 'Well?' 'That's the list of the folk from whom I buy. D'you see? Well, then, here on this page are the country folk, and the numbers after their names are where their accounts are in the big ledger.
